name: Marine War Risk Insurance
summary: Post-ceasefire: war risk insurance dipped to 0.35-0.45% of vessel value (from wartime peak 3.5-10%), but remain 7-10x pre-war levels. For $150M LNG carrier, still ~$500K-$675K per voyage. LMA: 88% of participants still have appetite at crisis premiums. Neil Roberts (LMA): 'highly unlikely that trade into the Gulf will simply resume.' Reinsurers exercised 72-hour cancellation clauses — restoring takes weeks. Gap: futures price ceasefire, insurance prices reality. Insurers are the best real-money probability assessment of ceasefire durability.

signals.content: Insurance companies began cancelling war risk coverage for vessels in Gulf. Marine war risk insurance prices rising 50-100%. (Source: CNBC, The National)

War risk insurance at 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage), 5x pre-crisis levels. Lloyd's JWC redesignated entire Arabian Gulf as conflict zone. Insurance premium IS the market's probability assessment — 'commercial risk logic has become an irregular warfare tool at Hormuz' per Irregular Warfare Journal. Hormuz tanker traffic collapsed 80%+. Commercial closure achieved through insurance costs making transit economically unviable, not through total military blockade. (Source: Lloyd's List, S&P Global, Irregular Warfare Journal)

WAR RISK INSURANCE UNCHANGED ON CEASEFIRE — Lloyd's: 'PHYSICAL NORMALISATION NOT COMPRESSED BY POLITICAL WILL.' Despite Brent crashing -13.5% on the ceasefire, hull war cover for Hormuz transit remains at $10-14M per voyage. Lloyd's: 'Even if a ceasefire takes hold, reinsurers must rebuild risk models from scratch. Each vessel seeking Gulf transit must be individually re-underwritten on a voyage-by-voyage basis. Pricing must clear across the entire reinsurance chain, from treaty reinsurer to P&I club to shipowner. Capacity that was withdrawn at the treaty level must be reinstated through negotiation, not announcement. This is a sequential, multi-party process that cannot be compressed by political will, executive order, or diplomatic breakthrough.' Implication: physical Hormuz flow restoration takes WEEKS even on a holding ceasefire. Brent's -13.5% move prices a faster restoration than is operationally possible.

Post-ceasefire: war risk insurance rates dipped to 0.35-0.45% of vessel value (from wartime peak 3.5-10%), but remain 7-10x pre-war levels. For a $150M LNG carrier, still ~$500K-$675K per voyage. LMA confirmed 88% of participants still have appetite. Neil Roberts (LMA): 'It is highly unlikely that trade into the Gulf will simply resume.' Reinsurers had exercised 72-hour cancellation clauses on clubs' charterers' liability at war start — restoring takes weeks, not days. Andrew James (Arthur J. Gallagher): underwriters 'already recognizing the ceasefire.' Gap: futures price ceasefire, insurance prices reality. Insurers are the best real-money probability assessment of ceasefire durability. (Source: TT News, CBS News, LMA, S&P Global)

edges.context: War risk insurance dipped to 0.35-0.45% but remains 7-10x pre-war. Rates dipped but nowhere near conflict-zone removal territory. Insurers are the best real-money probability assessment.
Insurance dipped post-ceasefire but remains 7-10x pre-war. 479 vessels stranded. Reinsurance restoration takes weeks. Frontline +3.3% ON oil crash = physical reality pricing.

Insurance constraint persists even on ceasefire — physical flow restoration lags political headlines. Brent futures at $96 vs physical at $120+ = $24 gap driven by insurance/shipping constraints that take weeks to normalize.

trigger_points.condition: Lloyd's JWC removes Arabian Gulf from conflict zone designation
trigger_points.mechanism: Insurance restoration is the binding constraint on physical Hormuz reopening. Until Lloyd's JWC formally lifts the conflict-zone designation, individual voyage underwriting persists at extreme rates. This is a more operationally meaningful trigger than the political ceasefire — the insurance market signals when normal commerce can actually resume.
